problem_summary: 2022 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy type), titled "The Coming Asteroids Mining, the Future Global Equity." The team had to (1) build a model to measure global equity across countries, (2) build a model of asteroid mining and analyze its impact on global equity, (3) analyze how changes in the asteroid-mining process alter global equity, and (4) recommend policy measures so asteroid mining advances global equity. The paper's central thesis is quantitative: using a coefficient-of-variation metric over a country-level welfare index, it shows asteroid mining (as initially structured, with few rich participants) widens cross-country dispersion and therefore worsens global inequity (asset CV 1.5332->1.5379, achievement CV 0.6912->0.6945), and that broadening participation reverses this while raising throughput worsens it.
models_used: Global Equity Assessment Model: a 3-level hierarchical indicator system (3 primary classes -- Asset, Achievement, Group differences; ~14 secondary classes; 25 tertiary indicators) over 205 countries, grounded in Amartya Sen's Capability Approach

Entropy Weight Method (EWM): objective weighting of the 25 indicators via information entropy e_j = -K*sum(p_ij*ln p_ij), K=1/ln(m), weights w_j proportional to (1-e_j); composite score S = 100*sum(y_ij*w_j)
Min-max normalization with benefit-attribute vs cost-attribute (forward) processing to make all indicators directionally comparable
Coefficient of Variation (CV = sigma/mu) of the Equity Index as the scalar global-(in)equity metric, compared across regions, years (2000 vs 2020), and mining scenarios

GE / McKinsey Matrix on the (GDP-per-capita, Aerospace-capability) plane to tier countries into mining vs funding roles; aerospace capability itself a 5-dimensional composite (government support, technological capacity, support capacity, industrial development, innovative development)
Asteroid Mining economic model: mass-collected formula M_i = rho*(4pi/3)*(D/2)^3*E_i*TP with throughput factor TP; total time T=T1+T2+T3; Net Present Value (NPV) with discount rate R
Cournot-Nash oligopoly equilibrium: linear demand p=a-bm, profit eta_i = p*m_i - c*m_i, equilibrium per-firm supply m_i=(a-c)/((n+1)b) to price the limited-supplier asteroid-resource market

Investment-decision model: ME-MI (Multiple Entrepreneurs to Multiple Investors) with decision variables minimum investment (I) and rate of return (ROR), plus a Hotelling-distance satisfaction measure dis=sqrt((xf-xi)^2+(yf-yi)^2) and a piecewise 'worth-investing degree' rule on ROR/I

Correlation test + Multiple Linear Regression: filter indicators with |R|>0.3 correlated to GDP, then regress ST, TR, EN, MR, PMS on GDP and Labor force to propagate the GDP shock from mining through the welfare index
Gaussian-noise sensitivity analysis: inject noise of increasing std-dev into raw data, recompute EWM weights, track relative weight error (under 5% for std-dev <= 0.8)
key_techniques: Reducing a fuzzy normative concept ('global equity') to a single defensible scalar: build a welfare index per country, then measure inequity as the cross-country dispersion (CV) of that index -- this makes every downstream question answerable as a delta in CV
Grounding the index in a citable theory (Sen's Capability Approach) rather than ad-hoc indicator picking, which gives the indicator hierarchy academic legitimacy
Objective weighting via Entropy Weight Method to pre-empt the 'you just chose weights to get your answer' judge critique; sensitivity analysis on those weights closes the loop

Two-stage country selection: GE matrix on (GDP, aerospace capability) decides WHO mines and WHO funds, cleanly separating Assumption 2 (few capable countries) into a quantitative tiering used consistently in Tasks 2 and 3

Linking the two models through a causal chain: mining -> revenue -> GDP shock to US/China/Europe -> regression-propagated changes in correlated indicators -> recomputed welfare index -> new CV. This is what makes Task 2 a genuine 'impact analysis' rather than two disconnected models

Using oligopoly theory (Cournot-Nash) as the justification engine: Assumption 2 (few suppliers) + irreplaceable supply + rational actors are explicitly invoked to legitimize the oligopoly framing, so the market model isn't arbitrary

Scenario knobs aligned to the prompt: Task 3 varies exactly two interpretable levers (throughput factor TP, number of participating countries) and reports their opposite-signed effects on CV, giving a crisp, quotable result
Concrete case anchoring: instantiating the abstract mining model on real asteroid 2011UW-158 with a numeric payoff (~$27B at TP=1/200) to make the model tangible and checkable
why_it_won: "It nailed the hardest part of a policy problem: making an unmeasurable concept (equity) measurable and then making every later claim a movement in that single number. Judges reward a clean metric-driven narrative, and this paper delivers one -- define equity, score 205 real countries, reduce inequity to a CV, and then show mining moves the CV. The modeling is broad and visibly grounded in named, citable frameworks (Sen's Capability Approach, Entropy Weight Method, GE/McKinsey matrix, Cournot-Nash oligopoly, Hotelling, NPV), signaling research maturity well beyond a typical team. Crucially, it closes the analytical loop the prompt demanded: the two models are causally linked (mining revenue -> GDP shock -> regression-propagated welfare change -> new CV), so Task 2 reads as real impact analysis, not two stapled-together models. It uses real data (World Bank, Our World in Data) and a real asteroid case (2011UW-158, ~$27B) for tangibility. Task 3 produces a clean, counterintuitive, quotable headline -- more participants improves equity, higher throughput harms it -- which is exactly the kind of crisp policy-relevant finding judges remember. And the objective EWM weighting plus a genuine Gaussian-noise sensitivity analysis pre-empts the obvious 'your weights drove your answer' objection. The result is a complete, self-consistent, well-cited arc from definition to data to model to policy."
section_highlights: "Summary: per-task structure that states the actual numeric results up front (the CV deltas), so a judge sees the punchline in 30 seconds. Section 3 (Model I): textbook build-up -- theory (Capability Approach) -> indicator hierarchy (Fig 4, 3-level/25-indicator table) -> normalization -> EWM weights (full weight table Fig 6) -> dual validation, both regional (asset/achievement heat maps) and historical (2000 vs 2020 CV trend cross-checked against the World Bank's World Development Report 2020). Section 4 (Model II): layered economic narrative -- who mines/funds (GE matrix), what to mine (C/S/X asteroid taxonomy), then the full pipeline cost->mass->time->NPV->responsibilities->oligopoly sale->impacts, capped by the 2011UW-158 case. Section 4.4: the standout linkage section -- correlation heat map filters indicators, multiple-linear-regression equations propagate the GDP shock, yielding the headline CV change. Section 5: tidy two-lever scenario analysis with bar charts (Fig 14/15). Section 6: policy organized into a clean restrict/encourage/supervise triptych mapped directly to the Outer Space Treaty, each tied back to a model result. Section 7: honest sensitivity curve (Fig 17). The per-task summary mapping and consistent 'result-as-CV-delta' framing are the strongest structural moves."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The headline result is statistically fragile: the entire 'mining worsens equity' conclusion rests on CV changing in the 4th decimal place (asset 1.5332->1.5379, achievement 0.6912->0.6945). Such tiny deltas are well within noise and the sensitivity analysis itself shows ~2-3% weight error -- judges could question whether the effect is real or rounding

Internal inconsistency / suspected copy-paste in the figures: the correlation heat map (Fig 13) has nearly identical rows repeated, and the EWM weight table mislabels Asset vs Achievement classes (e.g., GDP/LF/SA appear under 'Achievement Class', ST/ED/TR under 'Asset Class'), suggesting the published tables don't match the described logic

Notation sloppiness: weight formula indexes w_i but text says jth indicator; EWM Step 1 defines p_ij but Step 2 uses y_ij without defining the link; the NPV formula NPV = I/(1+R) - sum I_t/(1+R) lacks the time exponent (should be (1+R)^t) and is dimensionally suspect
Country-as-individual conflation: the model treats national aggregates as 'individual welfare' (acknowledged as a weakness), ignoring within-country inequality -- a serious gap for an EQUITY problem

Several key quantities are asserted rather than derived: cost 'about $3.0 billion', total time 'about 4 years', TP=1/200, and the $27B payoff are plugged in with thin justification; the Cournot equilibrium m_i=(a-c)/((n+1)b) is never actually instantiated with numbers
The ME-MI investment model and Hotelling distance are introduced but never quantitatively used to drive a result -- decorative modeling that adds citations but not analysis

GE-matrix inputs are coarse: the 5-dimensional aerospace scores are blocky integers (mostly 5/10/15/20/25) and several countries get identical vectors, so the country tiering is largely qualitative dressed as quantitative
Surface Area carries a 0.741 weight under the (mislabeled) class, an implausibly dominant weight that EWM produced mechanically and the team did not sanity-check or discuss
reusable_patterns: The 'index -> dispersion' pattern for any equity/fairness/inequality prompt: construct a per-entity composite score, then quantify (in)equity as a dispersion statistic (CV, Gini, Theil) of that score; every intervention becomes a delta in dispersion. Highly portable to future policy problems.

Three-level indicator hierarchy (primary class -> secondary theme -> tertiary measurable indicator) backed by a named theory and filled with real open data (World Bank, Our World in Data, UNDP). A clean, judge-friendly template for any 'build a measurement model' task.

Entropy Weight Method as the default objective-weighting tool, immediately paired with a Gaussian-noise sensitivity analysis on the weights -- a two-step combo that both produces weights and defends them.

Causal linkage recipe to connect two otherwise-separate models: shock one driver (here GDP), use a correlation filter (|R|>threshold) to find affected variables, fit a multiple linear regression to propagate the shock, then recompute the top-level metric. Turns 'two models' into 'one impact analysis.'
GE/McKinsey 2x2 matrix to classify actors on two normalized axes and assign them roles (who acts / who funds), with each axis itself a small composite sub-model.

Borrowing economics primitives off-the-shelf when a market exists: Cournot-Nash oligopoly for limited-supplier markets, NPV for multi-year payoffs, Hotelling distance for satisfaction/positioning -- a citation-rich toolkit for econ-flavored problems.

Result-first per-task summary: the abstract states each task's actual numeric outcome, and policy recommendations are each traced back to a specific model result (restrict/encourage/supervise triptych) -- a structural template that makes the paper easy for a judge to grade quickly.
Anchoring an abstract model in one concrete, named real-world case (asteroid 2011UW-158) with a single headline number to make the modeling tangible and falsifiable.
